note: The legendary main square; it's flat and rollable (Marrakech is a flat city), full of food stalls, musicians, snake charmers and henna artists - chaotic and magical. No cars. Watch for pickpockets and pushy performers. Uneven in patches.

Yves Saint Laurent's famous cobalt-blue botanical garden; wide paved accessible paths, benches, shaded cafe and the fully accessible Berber Museum. A tranquil, accessible escape from the medina - a highlight. Very busy at peak.

19th-century palace with stunning ornate courtyards and zellige tilework; offers limited/partial accessibility - some ground-level courtyards are reachable but there are thresholds and it sits in the harder-to-navigate medina. Take transport to the door.
16th-century royal necropolis with breathtaking tilework; features wheelchair ramps making the main areas accessible. Impressive and worth it; can get a queue in the narrow entry.

A restored historic riad-garden in the medina with fountains and Islamic-garden design; the main garden areas have wheelchair-accessible paths and assistance is available, though some older sections are uneven and the tower is not accessible. Peaceful.

The city's iconic 77m minaret; non-Muslims cannot enter but the exterior and surrounding gardens are admired from paved accessible paths - a lovely sunset spot. Grounds accessible, mosque interior closed to visitors.

The maze of covered markets (leather, spices, lanterns, textiles); atmospheric but genuinely hard - narrow, crowded, competitive, with steps into most stalls and rough patches. Doable in short bursts with a companion; not for the faint-hearted.
Ruined 16th-century palace with a photography museum inside; the vast open courtyard is partly navigable but the ruins have uneven ground and levels. Storks nest on the walls. Partly manageable.
Historic olive-grove gardens with a pavilion and reflecting pool framed by the Atlas Mountains; the main paths are flat and accessible. A calm, scenic, low-key stop.

The walled old city; Marrakech is flat and medina lanes are wider than in Fes, so with help it's navigable, but roads are rough, cars can't enter, and tourist infrastructure is only lightly adapted. A horse-carriage (caleche) is a popular accessible way to tour it.
